title: NodeSplittingHard

lean_statement.name: nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
lean_statement.code:
/-- The *single-world intervention graph (SWIG)* of the CDMG `G`
w.r.t. a set `W ⊆ G.V` of output nodes: the new CDMG over the
carrier `α ⊕ ↑W` obtained by first node-splitting on `W` (yielding
`w^o = Sum.inl w` and `w^i = Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩`) and then
hard-intervening on the freshly introduced `W^i = Set.range Sum.inr`
copies. See `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` definition
`def:G_node-splitting_intervention` (def 3.12 of the LN).

By construction this is exactly
`(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)`;
the four `@[simp]` lemmas `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J`,
`nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_V`,
`mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_E`,
`mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_L` below characterise the four
components and are the gateway for every downstream SWIG rewrite. -/
noncomputable def nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) : CDMG (α ⊕ ↑W) :=
  (G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn
    (Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W))

lean_statement.name: swig
lean_statement.code:
/-- Short-form alias for `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn`, exposing
the LN's `\swig` macro / Richardson--Robins SWIG terminology as
the dot-notation `G.swig W hW`. The alias is purely cosmetic:
its job is to let downstream Lean prose (claim_3_11 here, and the
counterfactual / identification / iSCM machinery of chapters
4 -- 16) read like the lecture notes' `G_{\swig(W)}` rather than
the eight-syllable long-form name.

Declared as a `noncomputable abbrev` (not `def`) so that the four
`@[simp]` lemmas attached to `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` fire
on `G.swig W hW` terms by reducibility; a plain `def` would have
forced every downstream caller either to `unfold swig` first or to
maintain a parallel `swig_*` simp-lemma family. -/
noncomputable abbrev swig (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    CDMG (α ⊕ ↑W) :=
  G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW

lean_statement.name: nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J
lean_statement.code:
/-- The *input* nodes of `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW`
are `Sum.inl '' G.J ∪ Set.range Sum.inr`: the original inputs
(carried under the canonical `inl` embedding) plus the fresh
intervention-input labels `W^i = Set.range Sum.inr`. This matches
the LN's `J_{swig(W)} = J ⊔ W^i` (def 3.12 item i.) under the
identification `α ≅ inl '' α` of `NodeSplittingOn.lean`. By
definition of the composition together with the `@[simp]` lemmas
`hardInterventionOn_J` and `nodeSplittingOn_J`. -/
@[simp] theorem nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_J
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).J =
      Sum.inl '' G.J ∪ Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W) := by

lean_statement.name: nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_V
lean_statement.code:
/-- The *output* nodes of `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW`
are `Sum.inl '' G.V`: the LN's `(V ∖ W) ⊔ W^o` collapses to all of
`Sum.inl '' G.V` under our identification `w^o = Sum.inl w` (so
`W^o ⊆ Sum.inl '' G.V` since `W ⊆ G.V`, and `(V ∖ W) ∪ W = V` when
`W ⊆ V`). Matches def 3.12 item ii. Proven by reducing to
`(Sum.inl '' G.V ∪ Set.range Sum.inr) \ Set.range Sum.inr` via the
NS / HI projection simp lemmas, then noting `Sum.inl '' G.V` is
disjoint from `Set.range Sum.inr` by constructor mismatch. -/
@[simp] theorem nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_V
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V) :
    (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).V = Sum.inl '' G.V := by

lean_statement.name: mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_E
lean_statement.code:
/-- *Directed-edge* membership in `G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn
W hW`: a pair `p` is a directed edge of the SWIG iff it is the
relabeling `(split1 W v₁, Sum.inl v₂)` of some original edge
`(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E`. Matches def 3.12 item iii.'s set-builder
`{v_1^i → v_2^o | v_1 → v_2 ∈ E}` (source dispatches on `v_1 ∈ W`
via `split1`; target is always the canonical `inl`). The post-NS
piece 2 (split edges `w^o → w^i`) of `nodeSplittingOn_E` is killed
by the HI because its target `Sum.inr w` lies in `W^i = Set.range
Sum.inr`; the post-NS piece 1's target `Sum.inl v₂` never does. -/
@[simp] theorem mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_E
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {p : (α ⊕ ↑W) × (α ⊕ ↑W)} :
    p ∈ (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).E ↔
      ∃ v₁ v₂, (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.E ∧ p = (split1 W v₁, Sum.inl v₂) := by

lean_statement.name: mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_L
lean_statement.code:
/-- *Bidirected-edge* membership in
`G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW`: a pair `p` is a bidirected
edge of the SWIG iff it is the double-`inl` relabeling
`(Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂)` of some original `(v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L`.
Matches def 3.12 item iv.'s `L_{swig(W)} = {v_1^o ↔ v_2^o | v_1 ↔
v_2 ∈ L}`. The HI on `W^i = Set.range Sum.inr` is a no-op on
`nodeSplittingOn_L` because both endpoints of every edge in the
post-NS `L` are of the form `Sum.inl _` (constructor mismatch with
`Sum.inr`). -/
@[simp] theorem mem_nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn_L
    (G : CDMG α) (W : Set α) (hW : W ⊆ G.V)
    {p : (α ⊕ ↑W) × (α ⊕ ↑W)} :
    p ∈ (G.nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn W hW).L ↔
      ∃ v₁ v₂, (v₁, v₂) ∈ G.L ∧ p = (Sum.inl v₁, Sum.inl v₂) := by

lean_explanation:
The Lean encoding mirrors the LN's four-item construction literally. `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type -- the LN's silent assumption that everything lives over some carrier -- and `G : CDMG α` is the input causal directed mixed graph $G = (J, V, E, L)$. The intervention set is `W : Set α` (the LN's $W \subseteq V$); `hW : W ⊆ G.V` discharges that subset hypothesis. The resulting SWIG lives over a new carrier `α ⊕ ↑W`: a disjoint union of the original vertex type with a fresh copy of `↑W` (Lean's subtype `{v : α // v ∈ W}`). The two `Sum` constructors play the LN's two roles: `Sum.inl w` is $w^o$ (canonical observation copy, identified with the original $w$) and `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩` is $w^i$ (fresh intervention-input label). The LN's set $W^i$ is then `Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W)`.

The main definition is a one-line composition: `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)` -- first node-split on $W$ to introduce the $w^i$ copies, then hard-intervene on those copies to make them inputs and strip every directed edge into them. A short-form `noncomputable abbrev` `swig` exposes the LN's $\swig$ notation as dot-notation `G.swig W hW`. Four `@[simp]` lemmas then characterise the LN's items i.)--iv.): `_J` gives $J_{\swig(W)} = J \sqcup W^i$ as `Sum.inl '' G.J ∪ Set.range Sum.inr`; `_V` gives $V_{\swig(W)} = (V \setminus W) \sqcup W^o$, which collapses to `Sum.inl '' G.V` once $w^o$ is identified with the original $w$; the membership lemma `mem_..._E` gives $E_{\swig(W)}$'s relabeling rule (source via `split1 W v_1`, target as `Sum.inl v_2`); `mem_..._L` gives $L_{\swig(W)}$'s double-`Sum.inl` form, since bidirected endpoints both lie on the observation side. The strict-implicit binder `{p : (α ⊕ ↑W) × (α ⊕ ↑W)}` on the membership lemmas lets `simp` instantiate the pair from context without forcing the caller to spell it out.
design_choices:
**Define the SWIG as the literal composition `nodeSplittingOn` followed by `hardInterventionOn`, not as a fresh field-by-field CDMG.** The composition `(G.nodeSplittingOn W hW).hardInterventionOn (Set.range Sum.inr)` *is* the LN's construction read item by item, not a rephrasing of it. Item i.) drops out of `hardInterventionOn`'s addition of `Set.range Sum.inr` to the inputs; item ii.) drops out of HI's removal of those fresh nodes from the outputs (the remaining observation side is exactly $W^o \sqcup (V \setminus W)$, identified with `Sum.inl '' G.V`); items iii.) and iv.) drop out of the post-split edge sets once HI deletes everything targeting $W^i$. A fresh field-by-field rebuild would have to re-prove the structural obligations (`disjoint_JV`, `E_subset`, `L_subset`, `L_irrefl`, `L_symm`, `disjoint_EL`) already discharged by the two building blocks, and would create a parallel API that every downstream composition with another node-splitting or hard-intervention would have to bridge.

**Intervention target `Set.range (Sum.inr : ↑W → α ⊕ ↑W)` is the post-split image of $W^i$.** After `nodeSplittingOn` lifts everything onto the carrier `α ⊕ ↑W`, the fresh intervention-input copies $W^i$ are exactly `Sum.inr`'s image of `↑W`. Hard-intervening on `Set.range Sum.inr` is therefore the literal LN sentence at the bottom of def 3.12: "turn all nodes of $W^i$ into input nodes, removing all edges into $W^i$". This is the *role-flipped* analogue of intervening on the *canonical* `Sum.inl` copies (as used in the commutativity result for disjoint hard interventions and node-splittings): there, the original-side $W$ carries the intervention; here, the fresh $W^i$ does. That asymmetry is what gives the SWIG its "single-world" character -- the original outputs (`Sum.inl '' G.V`) are preserved as observables while the fresh $W^i$ carries the intervention.

**Carrier `α ⊕ ↑W` and the `Sum.inl` / `Sum.inr` convention are inherited from `nodeSplittingOn`, not re-chosen here.** `Sum.inl w = w^o` (canonical observation copy, identified with the original $w$) and `Sum.inr ⟨w, _⟩ = w^i` (fresh intervention-input label) are fixed by the prior row, where the longer justification lives -- the LN's own hint $W = W^o$ together with the Richardson--Robins SWIG convention $X = X^o$. The four `@[simp]` lemmas exposed here speak in those terms (`Sum.inl '' G.J` in `_J`, `Sum.inl '' G.V` in `_V`, `split1 W v_1` and `Sum.inl v_2` in `_E`, double-`Sum.inl` in `_L`); a reader who skips the node-splitting design notes would otherwise be surprised by constructor names appearing inside LN-faithful set-builder forms.

**Expose a short-form `noncomputable abbrev swig` for the LN's $\swig$ notation, declared as `abbrev` rather than `def`.** The alias is purely cosmetic -- it lets downstream Lean prose read `G.swig W hW` the way the lecture notes read $G_{\swig(W)}$. Declaring it as `abbrev` rather than `def` means the four `@[simp]` lemmas attached to `nodeSplittingHardInterventionOn` fire on `G.swig W hW` terms by reducibility; a plain `def` would have forced every downstream caller either to `unfold swig` first or to maintain a parallel `swig_*` simp-lemma family.
